name: Rúben Dias
hints: Immediately transformed Manchester City's defence after joining for £61m in 2020
Won the Premier League Player of the Season award in his debut campaign — rare for a defender
Won three Premier League titles and the Champions League in four seasons
Captains Portugal and is considered the nation's best defender since Figo's era
Was brought up in the Benfica academy, the same system that produced Joao Felix

name: Gianluigi Donnarumma
hints: Was named Player of the Tournament at a major European championship
Made his debut for a famous Italian club at just 16, replacing a club legend
Left that club on a free transfer, causing outrage among fans who called him a traitor
At 1.96m, he is one of the tallest goalkeepers to play at the top level
Considered the long-term successor to a legendary Italian goalkeeper who shares his surname

name: Antoine Griezmann
hints: Won a World Cup in 2018 where he finished as the tournament's joint top scorer
Rejected Manchester United in a pre-announcement documentary before signing elsewhere
Won the Europa League twice with the same Spanish club across two separate stints
Named best player at a European Championship despite his team finishing runners-up
Was deemed too small by Spanish clubs as a teenager before being signed by a Basque club

name: Son Heung-min
hints: Became the first Asian player to win the Premier League Golden Boot in 2021/22
His father, a former semi-professional, trained him rigorously from childhood
Completed mandatory military service — a requirement for South Korean males — via tournament gold
Became the all-time record scorer for a north London club, surpassing a club legend
Scored a solo wonder goal against a Burnley side that is widely remembered as one of the PL's best

name: Bernardo Silva
hints: Won the Ligue 1 title at Monaco in the season before joining a Premier League giant
Named Manchester City's Player of the Season in 2021/22
Has won 5 Premier League titles, all at the same club
Despite never being a headline name, is consistently rated among the best midfielders globally
Rejected multiple La Liga moves to remain at his club — reportedly out of loyalty to the manager

name: Alexia Putellas
hints: Won back-to-back Ballon d'Or Féminin awards in 2021 and 2022
Tore her ACL on the eve of the 2022 European Championship yet still attended to support
Led her club to multiple UEFA Women's Champions League finals
Won the FIFA Women's World Cup in 2023, contributing to a historic Spanish victory
Widely considered the greatest women's footballer of her generation

name: Aitana Bonmatí
hints: Won the Ballon d'Or Féminin in consecutive years — 2023 and 2024
Named Best FIFA Women's Player in 2023
Was the standout performer in Spain's 2023 Women's World Cup winning campaign
Won the UEFA Women's Champions League multiple times with the same Catalan club
Born in Sant Pere de Ribes, she has spent her entire career at one club

name: Julian Álvarez
hints: Scored 4 goals in the knockout stages of the 2022 World Cup to help win Argentina's third title
Was technically on loan at a Premier League club during the 2022 World Cup
Won the Champions League and two Premier League titles in just two seasons
Left Manchester City for £81.5m to join a Spanish club in 2024
Nicknamed 'La Araña' (The Spider) for his tireless movement and work rate

name: Alisson Becker
hints: Set a world record fee for a goalkeeper when joining his current club for £65m in 2018
Scored a 95th-minute header from a corner to keep his club's title hopes alive in 2021
Won the Champions League, Premier League, and FIFA Club World Cup at the same club
His older brother Muriel is also a professional goalkeeper — a rare family trait
Won the Best FIFA Goalkeeper award in 2019

name: Alexander Isak
hints: Moved to Liverpool in 2025 for a fee that shattered the previous British transfer record
Idolised a famous Swedish striker growing up — and now holds Sweden's transfer record himself
Scored 25+ goals in a debut Premier League season despite joining a club outside the top 4
Came through Borussia Dortmund's academy before flourishing in Spain's top flight
Known for his elite first touch, movement, and composure in front of goal

name: Zinedine Zidane
hints: Scored two headers in a World Cup final at the age of 26 to win the tournament for his nation
Won the Champions League both as a player and later as a manager at the same club
His infamous head-butt in the 2006 World Cup final remains one of sport's most iconic moments
Won the Ballon d'Or in 1998, the same year he won the World Cup
Of Algerian Kabyle descent, grew up in a housing estate in Marseille

name: Ronaldinho
hints: Won the Ballon d'Or in 2005 at a Spanish club during one of football's most magical individual runs
Made Real Madrid fans stand and applaud him during an away league match
Won the World Cup with Brazil in 2002, setting up the iconic second goal
Was arrested and briefly imprisoned in Paraguay in 2020 over a passport issue
Known for his trademark smile and no-look passes that deceived entire defences

name: Thierry Henry
hints: Became a north London club's all-time record scorer with 228 goals
Was part of the 'Invincibles' — a side that went an entire league season unbeaten
Initially struggled as a winger at Juventus before being reinvented as a striker by Arsene Wenger
Won the 1998 World Cup and Euro 2000 with France as part of a legendary squad
Scored one of Wembley's greatest goals in the 2003 Community Shield with a solo run

name: Ronaldo Nazário
hints: Won the World Cup in 1994 and 2002, scoring in the final of the latter
Won the FIFA World Player of the Year award three times — 1996, 1997, and 2002
His career was repeatedly interrupted by serious knee injuries but he kept returning
Was nicknamed 'O Fenômeno' — widely considered the most complete striker in football history
Scored both goals in the 2002 World Cup final against Germany in Yokohama

name: Zlatan Ibrahimovic
hints: Scored a 30-metre overhead kick for Sweden against England in 2012 — often called the greatest international goal ever
Won league titles in the Netherlands, Italy, Spain, France, and England
Never won the Champions League despite playing for elite clubs across Europe
Famously dismissed his time at a Spanish club by saying there is only one king
Returned to AC Milan at 38 years old and scored 15 league goals in a title-winning season

name: Paolo Maldini
hints: Spent his entire 25-year professional career at a single Italian club
Won 5 European Cups / Champions League titles in an unmatched defensive career
His father Cesare also played for and managed the same club
Was never sent off in over 900 senior appearances — a statistical anomaly for a defender
Considered by many the greatest defender who ever lived, known for timing tackles perfectly

name: Gianluigi Buffon
hints: Holds the record for most Serie A appearances, playing until his mid-40s
Won the World Cup with Italy in 2006, conceding only two own goals in the tournament
Joined a French club at age 39 after an emotional farewell to his boyhood club
Won more league titles than any other player in Italian football history
Was bought by Juventus in 2001 for a then world record fee for a goalkeeper

name: Xavi Hernandez
hints: Won the World Cup in 2010 and back-to-back European Championships in 2008 and 2012
Made a record 767 appearances for a single Catalan club during the tiki-taka era
Was named man of the tournament at Euro 2008 and the 2010 World Cup
Famously had the best pass completion rate at every major tournament he participated in
Retired to Qatar before returning to manage his boyhood club

name: Andrés Iniesta
hints: Scored the winning goal in a World Cup final in extra time with his nation's first-ever win
Was named man of the tournament at the same World Cup despite not winning the Golden Boot
Spent 22 years at a Catalan club, departing for Japan in an emotional farewell
Known for his ability to find space in tight situations and never losing the ball
One of only a handful of players to win the World Cup, two European Championships, and the Champions League

name: Sergio Ramos
hints: Scored a 93rd-minute header in a Champions League final to force extra time and spark a famous comeback
Holds the record for most red cards in the history of the Champions League
Captained both his club and country to their most successful periods in history simultaneously
Won four Champions League titles, all at the same Spanish club
His long-time defensive partner was from France and equally regarded as among the best ever

name: Iker Casillas
hints: Was the goalkeeper for two European Championship wins and one World Cup with Spain
Made his Real Madrid debut at age 16 and went on to make over 700 appearances
His save in a 2010 World Cup semi-final against Arjen Robben is considered one of the greatest ever
Suffered a heart attack during training at Porto in 2019 that effectively ended his career
Captained the greatest international cycle in Spanish football history

name: Cafu
hints: The only player in history to appear in three consecutive World Cup finals — 1994, 1998, and 2002
Won the World Cup in 1994 and 2002, captaining Brazil in the latter
Revolutionized the right-back position as an attacking weapon rather than a pure defender
Spent his prime years in Serie A — first at Roma, then at a famous Milan club
Named in the FIFA 100 list of greatest living footballers compiled by Pele

name: Roberto Carlos
hints: Scored a free kick against France in 1997 that physicists initially said was scientifically impossible
Won the Champions League three times at a Spanish club where he spent 11 years
Holds the record for most appearances as a left back in Champions League history
Had thighs so powerful that his stride was measured to be almost horse-like in pace studies
Won the 2002 World Cup alongside another Brazilian legend also named Ronaldo

name: Rivaldo
hints: Won the Ballon d'Or and FIFA World Player of the Year in 1999 while at a Spanish club
Scored a bicycle kick goal in the last minute to qualify Brazil for the 2002 World Cup
Was part of the Brazil squad that won the 2002 World Cup — contributing goals in the knockout stages
Was fined by FIFA for feigning injury after being hit by a ball during a Turkish league match
Played at the top level until his late 30s, representing numerous clubs across Europe and beyond

name: Fabio Cannavaro
hints: Won the Ballon d'Or in 2006 — the last defender to win the award
Captained Italy to the 2006 World Cup, conceding only twice in open play
Joined Real Madrid the same summer, winning La Liga in his first season
Is considered one of the most complete centre-backs in football history despite being under 6ft
Made over 100 appearances for Italy, captaining them for the majority of his latter career
